AI CollaborationGovernance Framework

Most organisations have deployed AI. Almost none have answered the three questions that determine whether it actually works.

Work Sample
Demonstrates how I design an AI collaboration governance system — the framework that ensures every AI output is accountable, traceable, and safe to act on. Human authority remains the governing principle throughout.

Work Sample · Governance Framework
Applies To

Knowledge Architecture · Lucent Prism

1
Part One

Three Tensions Leadership Cannot Delegate

What research reveals about where senior leaders are actually struggling

Most organisations have deployed AI. Some have written a policy. Almost none have answered the three questions that determine whether AI actually works: Who owns the output when it is wrong? What knowledge informed it? And who decided it was safe to act on?

Research with senior executives across global enterprises reveals three tensions that are impossible to delegate — and that no technology vendor will solve. Leaders navigating AI adoption at scale are managing all three simultaneously, in real time, under pressure to demonstrate value.

01
Contested Value
What does "AI working" actually mean in your organisation? Leaders and teams have different answers. Without a shared definition of value, governance has no foundation — because you cannot govern what you cannot measure.
Source: HBR research, 35 global executives, Feb 2026
02
Continuous Disruption
Governance written for today's tool landscape is obsolete within months. The organisations that survive are not those with the most comprehensive policies — they are those whose governance architecture is designed to evolve. Structure that cannot adapt is not governance. It is documentation.
Source: HBR research, Senior Leaders Struggling with AI Adoption
03
Emotionally Divided Teams
Some people fear AI. Some race ahead. Leaders are managing both simultaneously. Research confirms: the human architecture around AI — how people relate to it, trust it, and take ownership of its outputs — determines whether AI delivers value or generates liability.
Source: HBR, Empathetic Leadership Can Make or Break AI Adoption, Apr 2026

"The capability is secondary to the leadership architecture around it. Companies that successfully integrate AI will prioritise leading human-centric transformation."

Why Governance Fails Before It Begins

Organisations treat AI governance as a technology problem. They configure access permissions and call it done. Access is not governance. Accountability is governance. The question is never whether the AI could produce the output — it is whether a named human being has reviewed, approved, and owns it.

2
Part Two

The Governance Architecture

Five dimensions — and the human thread running through all of them

A governance framework is not a policy document. It is a living architecture — structural and human simultaneously. The five dimensions below define what must be governed. The thread connecting them is the same in every case: human judgment, human accountability, human authority.

1
Output Ownership
Every AI output has a named human owner — accountable for its accuracy, appropriateness, and consequences. Not a department. One person, by name. Without this, accountability dissolves into the organisation and nothing is learned when something goes wrong.
2
Quality Standards
The criteria by which AI outputs are assessed before they are acted on or distributed. Not vague. Documented, measurable, and role-specific. Different output types require different standards — a client proposal is not governed the same way as an internal summary.
3
Knowledge Preservation
The institutional knowledge that informs AI prompts and validates AI outputs does not exist only in people's heads. It is captured, governed, and current. Poor knowledge in produces poor governance out — regardless of how sophisticated the tool.
4
Access & Authorisation
Who in the organisation may use which AI tools for which purposes — defined by role, not assumed by access. Escalation paths documented. This is not restriction. It is the structure that makes expanded AI use defensible when questions arise.
5
Audit & Accountability
A reviewable record of significant AI-assisted decisions: what was asked, what was produced, what human reviewed it, what action followed. Not surveillance. Accountability. The organisation that cannot trace its AI outputs cannot learn from them.
The human architecture is the sixth dimension — unnamed because it runs through all five. How people relate to AI, trust it, and take ownership of its outputs is what makes the other five work in practice rather than on paper.
The Governance Paradox

The organisations most in need of AI governance are those moving fastest — and therefore least likely to pause and build it. The framework's first deliverable is not a policy document. It is a maturity assessment that shows the organisation what it is already exposed to. That assessment is what creates the will to govern.

3
Part Three

The Decision Boundary

What AI may decide, advise, draft — and what must remain entirely human

The most consequential governance decision is not which AI tool to deploy. It is which decisions the AI may inform, and which must remain entirely human — regardless of what the tool is capable of. Without this map, the boundary is drawn retrospectively: after something goes wrong.

"The decision boundary is not drawn by AI capability. It is drawn by human accountability. The framework makes that boundary explicit before the tool is deployed — not after the first incident."

Decision Type AI Role Human Role Governance
Internal Knowledge SynthesisSummarising policies, research, meeting records, documentation
Drafts from authorised knowledge sources. Flags gaps and conflicts. Does not decide what is relevant.
Reviews accuracy. Approves distribution. Named owner recorded before sharing.
Standard
Client-Facing OutputProposals, reports, recommendations sent externally
Drafts and formats. Does not determine strategy, position, or commitment.
Full review mandatory. Owner signs off. Version and approver recorded.
Elevated
Policy and Governance DraftingInternal standards, SOPs, compliance documents
Structures and drafts to specified standard. Applies templates. Flags regulatory references.
Subject matter expert reviews content accuracy. Authority approves. Two-stage sign-off.
Elevated
People DecisionsPerformance, hiring, promotion, disciplinary matters
Data aggregation only, if explicitly authorised. No narrative, no recommendation.
Decision entirely human. AI involvement documented and disclosed where required by law.
Restricted
Legal, Regulatory, ComplianceAdvice, interpretation, filings, disclosures
Background research only. Does not interpret, advise, or conclude.
Qualified human authority owns all output. AI role never presented as advisory to regulators.
Restricted
What the Matrix Enables

The decision matrix is not a restriction on AI use. It is the structure that makes expanded AI use defensible. An organisation with a clear boundary can move fast with confidence. An organisation without one cannot — because it does not know where its exposure begins.

4
Part Four

From Assessment to Governed Operation

Where the organisation is — and what LP brings to closing the gap

Every LP engagement begins with a maturity assessment. Not a benchmark to aspire to — a diagnostic to act from. The assessment maps where the organisation is actually exposed, not where leadership believes it is.

L1
Unaware
AI is in use. Nobody has mapped it. No ownership. No audit trail. Exposure unknown.
L2
Acknowledged
Leadership knows AI is in use. Informal guidelines exist. Accountability is assumed, not assigned.
L3
Governed
Formal policy. Decision boundaries defined. Named owners assigned. Audit trail maintained.
L4
Measured
Governance health tracked. Output quality measured. Framework improves from evidence.
L5
Adaptive
Framework evolves as tools and use-cases change. Governance is a strategic capability.

What the Assessment Produces

Exposure MapWhere the organisation is most vulnerable to ungoverned AI output — by function, role, and consequence level.
Maturity PositionHonest placement at L1–L5 across each governance dimension. Not averaged. Mapped separately per dimension.
Priority ActionsThe governance gaps with highest consequence if unaddressed. Sequenced by risk, not by ease.
Programme DesignPhase-gated implementation calibrated to the organisation's current maturity and capacity for change.

The Depth This Demonstrates

A Note on This Document

This framework overview represents a window into LP's AI collaboration governance practice — not the full architecture. The knowledge systems, session governance, output ownership structures, and audit mechanisms that underpin this work are the product of sustained practice, not of framework design alone. LP does not offer governance frameworks it has not first built for itself.

In This Era
"AI has made it easier than ever to sound like an expert. What matters now is people with actual experience."
LP's Position
LP operates its own human-AI production practice at L4 across all five governance dimensions: named output ownership on every document, documented quality standards, knowledge preserved across sessions through a governed archive system, authorised access by task type, and a production register that maintains a complete audit trail.

The governance frameworks LP offers to clients have been tested in LP's own operations first. This is not a methodology on paper — it is a practice in use.

"AI without governance is not a capability. It is a liability with excellent marketing."
